meta.note: Talking points fleshed out from the full demo transcript (tools/sidekick-import); edit further in-app.

context.title: Discovery — Subcontractor Hub's situation (00:08–00:18)
facts: ~25 international employees paid directly today via international bank wires — per-wire fees on every payment (Nate).
Countries in play: Philippines, Mexico, South Africa (contractors today) + the Bulgaria team + 1 UK person paid to a US bank account.
Bulgaria team is currently a vendor: they invoice, individuals aren't paid directly.
A prior EOR-ish provider (~$20–25/person as contractors) was abandoned because it didn't integrate with TriNet.
Driver #1 (Justin, repeatedly): W2-style employment for Bulgaria — IP protection and stopping suspected payroll scraping (leadership told X, workers paid Y).
Priority order: Bulgaria EOR first; UK person also wanted as W2; other countries later.
Justin is 'hyper-focused' on Bulgaria being done right — decision-maker energy on this one thread.

context.title: EOR mechanics + rollout expectations (00:18–00:23)
facts: IP language is in the EOR employment contract; client can layer their own lawyer-reviewed IP agreement — Shane took that as an action item.
Employee onboarding is ~a two-week process (contracts, notifications, worker self-entry).
Staggered rollout (Bulgaria/international first) is possible; caveat: separate direct login until the rest lands.
Bulgaria team's current company is owned by its leaders (Peter, Alec) 'as far as we know' — notice-period risk considered low but unverified.

context.title: Pricing question (00:49–00:51)
facts: Justin asked cost directly; Shane: rates being finalized by country, 'not thousands — usually hundreds', quoted a $550–$700-ish PEPM range for EOR.
Consistent with the Tier-2 $550 SMB list price on the Bulgaria sheet; do NOT anchor below it.

context.title: Support model (00:56–00:58)
facts: Beyond the tech: named implementation manager plus human support for the employment itself (separations, compliance questions, country rules).
Positioning line worth keeping: 'It's more than payment — real people manage this employment for you.'

context.title: Wrap + committed next steps (00:58–01:01)
facts: Bryce owes: (1) presentable direct pricing — 'what are the economics for you guys'; (2) a deployment timeline.
Shane/Antaeus offered to field follow-ups on financials and contractual arrangement.
→ The Bulgaria pricing sheet we built is deliverable (1). Timeline doc is still open.

screens.title: Prism Global platform — home
screens.what: The admin landing view — one platform spanning contractors, EOR employees, and (later) your own subsidiary payroll, with the current pay periods segmented by worker type.
capabilities: One platform for **contractors, employer-of-record, and future direct/subsidiary** payroll
Pay periods segmented by worker type — contractor · one-time · hourly · fixed-rate · reimbursements
Technology platform **plus a human support layer** running alongside it
sp: One console covers every worker model a client might use — nothing to re-platform as they grow into new countries or hiring types.
de: Run contractors and full EOR employees from a single login; it still fits if you later stand up your own entity abroad.
screens.say:
This is the Prism Global platform — this is what all the international workers we're talking about get onboarded to, and how you interface with them.

It doesn't matter whether it's a contractor you're paying, someone on our employer-of-record, or, down the road, people you pay directly through your own subsidiary — this is the one platform to manage all of it.

There's the technology piece you're looking at, and a whole human support layer alongside it that I'll talk through at the same time.

The dashboard is where most people spend most of their time: pay periods broken out by employee segment — contractor payments, one-time payments, hourly, fixed-rate workers, reimbursements.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Confirm which landing view is shown (admin home vs. workforce overview).

screens.title: Pay period — all payment types in one view
navPath: Pay period — all payment types in one view
screens.what: The monthly pay-period review — a total-then-approve list for contractors, and a gross-to-net + employer-burden breakdown for EOR employees, grouped by country.
capabilities: Detailed view → running total → **approve** the run
EOR view surfaces **taxes, withholdings, benefits**
**Gross-to-net for the employee AND the employer burden** shown side by side
Broken out **by country**
sp: Employer burden is on the screen, not buried — makes the true all-in cost conversation concrete with the client.
de: See net pay and the employer's tax load per person before you approve — no surprises at invoice time.
branching: Bulgaria wasn't loaded in the demo set — Shane used **Ava in Canada** as the stand-in; flag that if a prospect asks to see their own country.
screens.say:
This contractor pay period shows every payment you make — one-time, hourly, fixed-rate, reimbursements — however each contract is set up. I open a detailed view, it totals it, and I approve it right here.

The employer-of-record view looks a little different: these are full-time regular employees in Bulgaria, so it brings up the taxes, withholdings, and benefits.

In my demo set I don't have Bulgaria loaded, but here's Ava in Canada — her wages, benefits, taxes, and it shows you the employee net, gross to net, and the employer burden.

Those two are the ones that matter: on, say, $10,000 a month, what are the additional taxes going to that local government. It's all broken out for you, by country.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Verify whether this is the payments list or an invoice detail; note the visible payment-type rows.

screens.title: Invoice breakdown by country — gross to net, no wire fees
navPath: Invoice breakdown by country — gross to net, no wire fees
screens.what: The pre-payroll invoice review: variance-check and correct, then approve — one US-originated invoice replaces per-employee international wires, with local-currency payout and local tax remittance handled for you.
capabilities: **Variance review + corrections** before anything hits payroll
**One US invoice, paid in USD to a US bank — no international wire fees**
We convert to local currency, pay employees' banks directly, and **remit taxes to the local authority**
Same flow in any country (CAD, GBP, etc.)
Reimbursements submitted + approved in-system; **Bulgaria = monthly cycle, end of month**
sp: Kills the per-wire fee model the client runs today and removes the 'money leaving the US to X country' footprint.
de: You touch one USD invoice; local currency, local banks, and local taxes are all handled — no wires, no FX headaches on your side.
branching: Bryce interjected: "stop us as we go if you have questions" — good place to invite the room in.
screens.say:
This is where you review before it goes to payroll — I can spot variances ("oh, that makes sense, we gave bonuses this month") and make corrections here first.

When I approve, it shows what we're paying employees in local currency — in an EOR model we're required to pay employees in local currency into their own bank account. Here's what's different from what you do now: you pay us in USD.

We send an invoice with your Bulgarian employees' salaries in USD, you pay us into our US bank — no international wire fees. We convert, send it directly to their bank accounts, and pay the taxes to the Bulgarian authorities.

No wires going back and forth from you — you originate and pay in the United States. Same in any country: Canadian dollars, UK pounds, whatever the local currency is, we convert and pay it.

Reimbursable expenses get submitted through the system, you set who approves, and Bulgaria runs a monthly payroll cycle, paid at the end of the month.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Confirm the by-country grouping and a per-employee drill-in are on screen.

screens.title: Time off — statutory leave honored per country
navPath: Time off — statutory leave honored per country
screens.what: The time-off surface — statutory leave enforced per country, an at-a-glance 'who's out' on the dashboard, and the team/HR view spanning contractors and EOR down to each worker's profile and signed contract.
capabilities: **Country statutory leave enforced** (vacation, holidays, maternity) — offer more, never less
Dashboard 'who's out **today / tomorrow**' + reminders
Team view across **contractors and EOR** in one place
Full **HR profile** per worker, incl. the signed contract
sp: Compliance is automatic, not the rep's burden — the platform holds the statutory floor for every country.
de: You can't accidentally under-provide leave; the country minimum is baked in, and you still control anything above it.
screens.say:
Time-off requests live here. Bulgaria has a statutory amount of paid vacation, holidays, leaves, maternity — things an employer must provide, and we always honor the country-specific minimum.

You can always offer more, but we can never offer less than the Bulgarian standard, even if that's a lot more than we're used to in the US. Back on the dashboard I can see who's out today and tomorrow and any reminders.

If I go to my team, it shows everyone across both populations — contractors and EOR — and I can drill into an employee's personal info; this is our HR system.

I can pull up Ava, view her HR profile, and see her signed employment contract, which is between the employee and PrismHR.

screens.title: Worker documents — the employment contract chain
navPath: Worker documents — the employment contract chain
screens.what: The worker's documents — the EOR contract chain (employee ↔ PrismHR, then PrismHR ↔ your company), where IP protection lives on both contracts, versus contractors who carry no statutory leave/benefits.
capabilities: Signed **employment contract on the worker record** (employee ↔ PrismHR as EOR)
**Company-to-company assignment agreement** to your business
**IP protection on both** the employment contract and the company agreement — reviewable by your counsel
Contractors: contracted-amount payment only, no statutory leave/benefits
sp: Directly answers the #1 driver on this deal — IP protection — with contract language on both sides.
de: Your IP is protected through the employee's contract and your agreement with PrismHR; layer your own lawyer-reviewed IP addendum on top if you want.
branching: Justin's core concern is IP/W2 — this screen is where you plant that; Shane took an action item to let them add their own IP agreement.
screens.say:
Her signed contract is her employment contract, and it's between the employee and PrismHR — because we're the local employer of record. The contract we have with you then assigns her to work on your behalf.

IP protection sits in the employment contract with the employee directly, and conversely our company-to-company agreement carries IP protection as well — you can review both with your attorneys. That's how the protection works.

I can pull up her contract right here. Contractors, by contrast, aren't regular full-time employees — you're just paying them a contracted amount, no statutory leaves or benefits — but we still manage the payment here.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Check whether a worker profile/documents screen is visible or this is spoken over another view.

screens.title: Contractor payments — wallet-funded, no statutory load
navPath: Contractor payments — wallet-funded, no statutory load
screens.what: Contractor payment handling (contracted amount, wallet-funded, flat PEPM) plus the live Q&A on country statutory costs — UK pension, social charges, and optional supplemental benefits.
capabilities: Contractor payments at the **contracted amount**, wallet-funded, **flat per-employee-per-month** fee (no per-wire fees)
**Onboarding status** per worker (signed / waiting on us)
Country statutory costs defined per country — **UK required pension + national healthcare via taxes**
**Optional supplemental benefits** (private-hospital access), employer-absorbed or cost-shared
sp: Reframes 'cheap contractor wires' into a predictable PEPM, and pre-empts the 'what else do we owe per country' question with real specifics.
de: You'll know exactly what each country requires (e.g. UK pension) and can add supplemental benefits on top when the statutory floor isn't enough.
branching: **Renee asked:** does a country add mandatory pay items (she cited Mexico housing/food stipends) for UK/Bulgaria? → Social charges always; Bulgaria specifics to be confirmed; UK has a required pension.
**Renee asked:** are benefit providers already set up? → Yes — national healthcare via social charges; optional supplemental on top (India weak, Australia public system good).
Objection-defuser Shane used: "none of this is free — they're paying a lot of taxes" when someone raises 'free healthcare.'
screens.say:
Contractors don't get statutory leaves or benefits — you're paying a contracted amount, and we manage the payment. Important: those payments aren't per-wire into each bank account.

You pay into a wallet, and it flows from there — so no per-wire fees, just a per-employee-per-month fee that manages all of it (I'll show the wallet later).

The onboarding tab shows where each person is in the process — have they signed, are they waiting on us.

On benefits and country add-ons: every company pays the social charges per employee, and we'll confirm any Bulgaria-specific allowances.

In the UK there's a required pension — a national pension program instead of a 401k — plus national healthcare through taxes. Healthcare abroad isn't free; it's paid through those social charges.

Most companies then add optional supplemental benefits so people can use private hospitals; you can absorb it or share the cost.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Confirm what's on screen during the benefits/social-charges Q&A (moments 29–30) — did the view change?

screens.title: Add a worker — employee (EOR) vs. contractor
navPath: Add a worker — employee (EOR) vs. contractor
screens.what: The fork at the start of add-worker: employee (EOR) vs contractor, and within contractor the plain-payment path vs Contractor Plus (PrismHR as agent of record, with a classification check).
capabilities: **Employee (EOR)** and **contractor** paths
Contractor = payment only (your contract, or a sample); **Contractor Plus = PrismHR is agent of record** with a **classification process**
**Misclassification risk analysis** — advice on fines/penalties per country
Client decides the risk; platform advises
sp: Gives the partner a compliance-forward story: you're not just moving money, you're keeping the client out of a misclassification mess.
de: Pick the model per worker; if a 'contractor' really behaves like an employee, you get a clear read on the risk before you decide.
branching: **Bryce/Shane clarified for the room:** once PrismHR flags someone as likely an employee under Contractor Plus, you can't then knowingly pay them as a contractor — but the choice of model is still the employer's.
Shane's line: "it's not an exact checklist — three or four questions and it's leaning one way; we just make sure you have full information."
screens.say:
Two roads when you add someone: employee or contractor. Contractor has two flavors.

Plain contractor is just payments — the contract is between you and the contractor (we have sample contracts, or use your own); we're only the method of payment.

Contractor Plus is where we become the agent of record — the contract is between the contractor and PrismHR — and there we run a classification process, because now our name and liability are on it.

If they don't meet the contractor requirements, we'll say we can't be party to it and they'd need to go employer-of-record.

If they're acting exactly like an employee, we'll show you the real risks — fines, penalties, real consequences if the government finds out — but ultimately it's your decision how much risk to take. For Bulgaria we'll choose employee.

screens.title: Where they live is where they're employed
navPath: Where they live is where they're employed
screens.what: The residency / work-authorization step — country of employment is set by where the worker physically lives (not nationality), with a work-authorization check the team verifies.
capabilities: **Country of employment = country of residence** (nationality is separate)
**Work-authorization prompt** (own permit?) with EU/Schengen handling
**Team-verified** documentation — not taken on the worker's word
sp: Surfaces a real compliance trap (a 'Bulgarian' actually in Spain) before contracts are cut — exactly the kind of thing that burns unmanaged setups.
de: The one worker possibly in Spain gets set up under Spanish rules automatically; you won't misfile them under Bulgaria.
branching: **Live on the call:** Renee said one of the ~12 likely lives in Spain; nationality vs residence was unknown and Renee took an action to confirm. This is the open item on the deal — residency drives the seat's country + cost.
screens.say:
Where they live is where they need to be employed. So the nationality says Bulgaria, but if one of them lives in Spain, I choose Spain — because that's where they live.

Then it asks: is the employee authorized to work in Spain — do they have their own work permit? As long as their work authorization allows it (and Bulgaria/Spain are both in the Schengen area), we're good, and I confirm they're authorized.

But our team is notified to verify it — we don't just take their word; we make sure the proper documentation and authorization exist. All of this is to onboard them correctly in the country where they actually live.

screens.title: Role setup — title, description, start window
navPath: Role setup — title, description, start window
screens.what: The role-details step — title, description, divisions/teams, location, start date (the ~2-week window), gross salary, and indefinite vs fixed-term contract.
capabilities: Role **title, description, divisions, teams, location** (office/remote)
**Start date with a ~2-week window** covering contract prep + country registrations (e.g. France/Philippines doctor visit)
Gross salary entry
**Indefinite vs fixed-term** contract, with local renewal-to-indefinite rules noted
sp: The 2-week window sets realistic go-live expectations — useful when the client wants a fast Bulgaria rollout.
de: Standard role setup; just know the start date is ~2 weeks out because the local contract and any registrations happen first.
branching: If they push for a faster start, anchor on the 2-week window = contract + country requirements, not platform lag.
screens.say:
For the Bulgarian nationals living in Bulgaria, work authorization is set, so it's a simple process.

You add the role — these are project managers, mid-level — a description of what they'll do, assign divisions and teams (engineering, operations, sales), and a location (city, office, or remote).

Then the start date: if we did this today, two weeks out is the 21st — that two-week window is what it takes to work the employment contract and clear any country requirements (some countries need registrations or waiting periods; in France and the Philippines they even have to see a doctor first).

Then the gross salary — we'll use $10,000 — and the contract type: indefinite (ongoing) versus fixed-term (a set period).

Most companies use indefinite; and note that after one or two fixed-term renewals it usually becomes indefinite anyway by local rule.

screens.title: Comp extras — recurring allowances + country PTO
navPath: Comp extras — recurring allowances + country PTO
screens.what: The comp-extras step — recurring allowances (e.g. mobile), country PTO you can add to but not below, and a built-in local-currency conversion check on the salary.
capabilities: **Recurring allowances** (e.g. $100 mobile) applied every paycheck
**Country PTO floor** shown; add extra days (e.g. birthday off)
Holiday-vs-required-work nuance flagged for compliance
**Currency check** — e.g. 10,000 BGN ≈ 5,500 USD — confirm the real amount
sp: Small, tangible flexibility (allowances, extra days) that makes the client feel in control while you hold the compliance line.
de: Add perks freely on top of the legal minimum; the currency check keeps you from mis-entering the salary.
screens.say:
I can set up additional recurring payments — say a $100 monthly mobile allowance — and it hits every paycheck going forward.

On vacation and PTO, we give you exactly how many days Bulgaria allows; we can't go below it, but you can add more — if you give everyone their birthday off, I add a day and it shows up.

One nuance: if it's a Bulgarian holiday but you're still working in the US and require them to work, that opens vacation/holiday-pay and jurisdiction questions — the point is we keep you compliant with Bulgarian law.

And there's a nice currency check: it flags that 10,000 Bulgarian is about 5,500 USD a month — so I can confirm the amount is right.

screens.title: Equipment — third-party provisioning, not US shipping
navPath: Equipment — third-party provisioning, not US shipping
screens.what: The equipment + benefits step — in-country third-party IT provisioning (not US shipping), and optional supplemental health tiers at a flat PEPM.
capabilities: **Third-party IT catalog** delivered in-country (avoids customs) — or personal device + reimbursement
Supplemental benefit tiers: **standard / premium / premium-plus-maternity** (global plans)
**~$210 PEPM** supplemental health; absorb, cost-split, or dependents at the same rate
sp: Two friction points (getting them a laptop, giving them 'real' benefits) solved without the client sourcing anything locally.
de: Kit them out through a local menu instead of exporting hardware, and add private-hospital-grade benefits for a flat monthly rate.
screens.say:
Equipment policy: the least effective way is shipping laptops and monitors from the US — they'll probably get caught in customs and it gets expensive.

Better, we have a third party you work with directly — a menu of IT equipment sent to their home or office. Or the new hire uses their personal computer and gets reimbursed.

On benefits — this is the question from earlier — part of Bulgaria's social charges are statutory benefits through the government, but you can offer more: standard, premium, premium-plus-maternity.

They're global plans and reasonable — about an extra $210 per employee per month if you choose it. You can absorb it, split it, and dependents are the same $210.

screens.title: Benefits — supplemental health at a flat PEPM
navPath: Benefits — supplemental health at a flat PEPM
screens.what: The benefits-selection + submit step — supplemental health via SafetyWing at a flat PEPM, then submission routes to a human implementation manager for the region.
capabilities: **SafetyWing** global healthcare network powering supplemental benefits
**$210 PEPM**, employer-absorbed / cost-split / dependents same rate
Submit routes to a **human implementation manager (Europe)** — 'AI-driven, but a human is involved'
sp: The 'a real person owns this' beat lands here — differentiates from pure-software EOR tools.
de: Named global benefits carrier, flat pricing, and a human who checks your submission before anything goes out.
screens.say:
Supplemental health is an extra $210 per employee per month if you choose it — absorb it, split it, or the employee covers dependents at the same rate.

Clicking in shows the coverages: we use SafetyWing, a global healthcare network, so we can manage supplemental benefits across the globe. I select it, and we're done — that's how you onboard an employee.

When I hit Submit, all of it goes to an actual human — a lot of what we do is AI-driven, but we keep a human in the loop — specifically one of our implementation managers who covers Europe.

screens.title: Offer letter — generated, client-reviewed, then sent
navPath: Offer letter — generated, client-reviewed, then sent
screens.what: The offer-letter step — human-generated from the hire data, client-reviewed before it reaches the worker, then e-signed and converted into the finalized employment contract.
capabilities: **Offer letter generated by the implementation manager**, then **client-reviewed before sending**
Contains salary + benefits; back-and-forth resolved at the offer stage
Employee **e-signs in their own Prism Global instance** → **employment contract finalized** with start date
Employee enters bank + private info during their step
sp: A clean approval gate the client controls — nothing goes to their worker without their sign-off.
de: You approve the exact offer before the employee ever sees it; they self-enter banking details, not you.
screens.say:
The implementation manager verifies everything's correct and generates the offer letter — and you review it before we send it to the employee. It contains their salary, any additional benefits, everything you discussed.

Any back-and-forth or questions happen at the offer-letter stage.

Once it's signed, the employee gets an email into their own instance of Prism Global to sign it; then we finalize the employment contract, which dictates their employment and start date.

That's also where the employee enters their bank details and private information.

screens.title: What the worker sees — payslips, time off, approvals
navPath: What the worker sees — payslips, time off, approvals
screens.what: The worker's self-service portal — payments, payslips, time-off requests with configurable approvals, optional time tracking, and reimbursements routed for correct tax handling.
capabilities: Payslips + full **payment history**
**Time-off requests** with configurable approval chain (manager → HR)
**Optional time tracking** (per day / per project)
**Reimbursements in-system** for correct tax accounting
sp: A polished self-service portal means fewer 'where's my payslip' pings back to the client and to you.
de: Your workers self-serve payslips, leave, and expenses — with approvals you control and tax handled correctly.
screens.say:
This is the employee's own view: every payment, how much they've been paid, where their payslips live.

If they need time off, they go right here — they see their holidays and available leave and request it, and you set the approval chain (frontline manager first, HR second, however you like).

If you need time tracking — hours per day or per project — you can turn it on; if you don't, you skip it.

And reimbursements — took a client to lunch, needs a monitor — go through the system so we account for them correctly and handle any taxes owed. That's the employee dashboard.

screens.title: Onboarding tracker — from offer letter to signed
navPath: Onboarding tracker — from offer letter to signed
screens.what: The onboarding status board — each in-flight hire and its stage (awaiting offer letter → signed), with workers self-uploading documents that HR verifies before the contract issues.
capabilities: **Per-hire status** (awaiting offer letter → agreement signed)
**Workers self-upload** passports / government docs (you don't collect them)
**HR verification** of documents — incl. the Spain worker's work authorization — before the contract issues
sp: Visible pipeline + self-serve document collection = far less chasing for both you and the client.
de: Track every hire's stage at a glance; your people upload their own documents and PrismHR verifies before anything is finalized.
branching: Ties back to the Spain worker (screen 8): their work authorization is verified here before the contract issues.
screens.say:
On the onboarding tab — here's Laurent in Bulgaria, awaiting his offer letter. That's with our implementation manager; they get it ready and send it, and then it flips here when the agreement's signed. Do it once and it's easy.

The employee uploads all their own information — you don't collect passports or government documents; they upload straight to the system for our HR managers to approve.

The person in Spain would upload their work authorization so we can verify they can truly work there, and we verify all of it before we issue the employment contract.

screens.title: Reports — workers and gross-to-net by country
navPath: Reports — workers and gross-to-net by country
screens.what: The reporting surface — workforce composition, EOR gross-to-net and per-country spend, GL export with codes, and variance, all exportable and split by worker type.
capabilities: **Worker report** (contractor vs EOR counts, monthly)
**EOR gross-to-net** + per-country total comp / taxes / bonuses
**Exportable to CSV / Excel**
**General-ledger report + GL codes** for import into your accounting
**Variance report**; everything split contractor vs EOR
sp: Finance-ready outputs (GL, exports) shorten the client's internal approval — fewer 'can it feed our books' blockers.
de: Pull workforce, gross-to-net, and GL-ready exports yourself — it drops straight into your accounting.
screens.say:
A couple of reports worth knowing. The worker report shows, month by month, how many workers you have — contractors, EOR, etc.

The EOR report gives you gross-to-net, and per country what you're spending on total compensation, taxes, bonuses. These are exportable to CSV or Excel.

There's a general-ledger report you can generate and import into whatever GL you use — and you can set up the GL codes here if they aren't already. And the variance report we glanced at earlier.

They all break out by contractor vs employer-of-record.

screens.title: Compensation calculator — true employer cost per country
navPath: Compensation calculator — true employer cost per country
screens.what: The compensation calculator (Tools & Resources) — salary in → employer all-in cost (incl. contributions/pension) and employee net take-home, per country.
capabilities: **Employer all-in cost** per country (e.g. UK £11.5k salary → **~£13.2k** with NI + pension)
**Employee net take-home** (UK example: **~£6.8k** after tax/pension/insurance)
Pre-hire budgeting before any commitment
sp: This is the tool that turns 'what will it really cost' into a number on screen — powers the direct pricing Bryce owes them.
de: Model the true employer cost and the worker's net for any country before you commit to a hire.
branching: **⚠ Bulgaria was not loaded in the calculator during the demo — Shane flagged he'd fix it.** He demoed the UK (and started Australia) instead.
screens.say:
Tools and resources — the compensation calculator is really nice. If I want to know exactly what it costs to hire someone in a country, I enter the salary and it shows what the employer pays all-in and what the employee gets.

Bulgaria isn't loaded yet — I'll get that fixed — so let's use the UK: at £11,500 salary, the total employer cost with National Insurance and the pension comes to about £13,200.

Then you see the net salary — with the UK's taxes, pension, and insurance, that £11,500 take-home is about £6,800. So right away you get a clear picture of the real cost and the real take-home.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Bulgaria was missing from the calculator during the demo (Shane flagged it) — note which countries were shown (Australia, UK).

screens.title: The wallet — contractor payout without wires
navPath: The wallet — contractor payout without wires
screens.what: The contractor wallet — client funds it once in USD; workers hold a US-stablecoin balance and withdraw via bank ($1–3), GCash, crypto, or a virtual card — replacing per-payment wires and their fees on both sides.
capabilities: **One flat PEPM fee, unlimited transfers** (pay weekly or monthly) — Bryce
US-stablecoin wallet; withdraw to **bank ($1–3, client pays none)**, **GCash**, crypto, or a **virtual credit card**; Wise transfers
**Hold in USD stablecoin** when the local currency is unstable; **3.29% US savings** + invest options
**US-account to US-account** — no international-transfer scrutiny / permanent-establishment signaling
Contractors elsewhere pay **$40–50 per withdrawal**; this replaces that
sp: The wallet is the wedge for the contractor book (Philippines/Mexico/South Africa) — unlimited transfers at one fee is a clean displacement of their wire model.
de: One monthly fee, pay as often as you like, and your contractors get their money instantly (or hold USD) with $1–3 fees instead of $40–50.
branching: **Today the wallet is contractors-only** — EOR employees must be paid in local currency to local banks; team is exploring EOR support. Don't over-promise it for the Bulgaria EOR seats.
Bryce co-sold the two advantages (flat fee/unlimited transfers; instant withdrawal + USD hold) — a natural partner tag-team moment.
screens.say:
Let me show you the wallet — this impacts the contractors and I think you'll like it.

Right now you're paying wires and wire fees across the globe, which is expensive and also signals to those countries that you're paying people there — which can start permanent-establishment risk.

Instead, with our contractor model you pay us in US dollars, and employees are paid into a wallet — think a Venmo-style wallet tied to a US stablecoin, all digital. The contractor holds their money there until they want it.

They can withdraw a number of ways: transfer to their own bank (conversion happens then, and it's a $1–3 fee — you as the client pay none of it); deposit to a mobile wallet like GCash in the Philippines; buy crypto; or spin up a digital/virtual credit card tied to the wallet to buy things online; they can even move money to Wise.

Bryce's two big advantages: one flat fee per month with unlimited transfers — pay weekly or monthly, your choice — and on the employee side, instant withdrawal, or they can hold funds in US-dollar stablecoin if their local currency is unstable.

They can even earn 3.29% in a US savings account or invest.

Contractors elsewhere get hit with $40–50 just to get their own money out; this is a per-employee-per-month fee no matter how often you pay, and it's US-account to US-account, so no international-transfer scrutiny.

Today it's contractors only — EOR still has to pay local currency into local banks — though our team is looking at bringing the wallet to EOR down the line.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Wallet may deserve its own module (not in the current 13). Confirm which wallet screens are shown: balance, withdrawal, digital card, stablecoin.
